region_of_origin: India (North Indian tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers shrimp/prawn curry as a dish class; used as closest defensible match for village-style prawn curry.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a prawn-based curry dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Indian prawn curry as a composite dish; A0CKM (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible single-ingredient anchor. Composite dish coding is not available in FoodEx2 at this specificity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for prawn curry as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not routinely code composite ethnic curry dishes at this level of specificity.

main_ingredients: Indian spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of village prawn curry is approximately 300 g, comprising shell-off prawns in a rich onion-tomato-spice gravy, served as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn curry, Indian style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), composite dish values; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) for micronutrient detail.
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for prawn curry (Indian style) as a composite dish. Macronutrient values are taken from that reference. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins) are supplemented from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data (FDC ID 175180) and scaled to the composite dish context. Sodium is estimated from typical Indian curry preparation using ICMR-NIN guidance on added salt and spice blends. All values represent a per-100 g edible portion of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of seafood curry dishes — analogous estimate based on prawn/shrimp curry GI data; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. University of Sydney GI Database entry for seafood-based curry dishes.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for village prawn curry specifically. GI is estimated at 25 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database and published international GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) for analogous seafood-based curry dishes with low carbohydrate content. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (3.2 g/100 g), yielding a very low glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_per_serving_g / 100 = 25 × (3.2 × 3.0) / 100 = 25 × 9.6 / 100 = 2.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) from added salt and spice blends in curry preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g per serving and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 300 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~25), resulting in minimal postprandial glucose impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Village prawn curry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the high protein content (11.2 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability, though sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content from prawns (~130 mg/100 g) and moderate sodium load from curry preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally higher in dietary cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation (150–200 g portions), noting that the low saturated fat content (0.9 g/100 g) and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fats are partially offsetting cardiovascular risk factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Village Prawn Curry is a North Indian-style seafood curry dish consisting of shell-off prawns cooked in a rich, aromatic gravy made from onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, and a blend of Indian spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, and garam masala. The dish is sautéed and then simmered to allow the prawns to absorb the flavours of the spice-laden gravy. The texture is tender and succulent, with the prawns retaining a slight firmness, while the gravy is thick, deeply coloured from turmeric and chilli, and intensely savoury with a moderate to high spice level. In Singapore, this dish is served at North Indian restaurants and is commonly paired with basmati rice, naan, or roti. Nutritionally, village prawn curry is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with 11.2 g protein and only 3.2 g carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The glycemic index is estimated at 25 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 300 g restaurant serving is approximately 2.4, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. Sodium content is moderate to high (~420 mg/100 g) due to added salt and spice blends, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Prawns are a natural source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine, contributing to neurological health and thyroid function. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal-certified in Singapore Indian restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn curry?
Is Indian prawn curry good for diabetics?
Can I eat prawn curry if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of prawn curry?
What spices are in Indian prawn curry?
How much sodium is in prawn curry?

confidence_reason: Macronutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for Indian-style prawn curry as a composite dish, which is the closest approved primary source for this cuisine type. Micronutrient values are supplemented from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data. No exact match for 'village prawn curry' exists in any approved database; the adaptation is defensible and clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed international GI tables, as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category, and prawn curry as a composite dish does not have a reliable vitamin D value in USDA SR Legacy either; vitamin D content of prawns is generally negligible. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as Indian prawn curry prepared with vegetable oil does not contain industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fats from the prawn itself are below detection threshold (trace). Nutrient values represent the finished dish per 100 g edible portion. Serving size of 300 g is estimated based on typical North Indian restaurant portion sizes in Singapore as observed across the three listed restaurants (Mirch Masalah, Khushboo, Curry Village). Price data (SGD 24.90) is preserved from the field survey data source record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source. The 'Chef Recommendation' qualifier in the original food name is a menu descriptor and has been removed from the canonical food_name to avoid promotional language; it is retained in the aliases list. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 (shrimp curry dish) is used as a closest_parent match. FoodEx2 A0CKM (cooked crustaceans) is the closest_parent defensible single-ingredient anchor; no composite Indian curry dish code exists in FoodEx2 at this specificity. SNOMED CT does not code this dish.
